nativeRange.habitat: Anthelia is found on shallow tropical reef flats, lagoons, and reef slopes, where it encrusts hard substrate, dead coral rubble, and rock surfaces in moderate-flow zones, typically at depths of 1 to 22 metres.

decorPreferences: placement away from adjacent corals to manage spreading
open substrate areas where colony spread can be monitored

diet.notes: Anthelia meets most of its nutritional needs through photosynthesis, carried out by symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae) living within its tissues, so target feeding is not generally required. The coral may benefit from dissolved organics present in an established reef tank, or from very occasional additions of phytoplankton or amino acids, though it relies far more on light than on active particle capture.

goodWith: other soft corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: adjacent corals without buffer zones — Anthelia encrusts rapidly and can smother slower neighbors
torch corals and hammer corals — their sweeper tentacles can sting Anthelia

summary: Anthelia is an encrusting soft coral (family Xeniidae) whose feathered polyps sway gently in current but do not pulse — a key distinction from the closely related Xenia. It is generally considered one of the more beginner-friendly soft corals and is known for spreading rapidly across rockwork.
careNotes:
Anthelia vs. Xenia — telling them apart: Anthelia is frequently confused with Xenia because both belong to family Xeniidae and share the eight-pinnate (feathered) tentacle structure common to all octocorals. The clearest distinguishing feature is motion: Anthelia polyps sway gently in ambient current but do not exhibit the rhythmic pumping action — the characteristic 'pulsing' — that defines Xenia and its relatives. Each Anthelia polyp bears exactly eight slender, plume-like tentacles growing from a tubular stalk, and the polyp stalks are typically described as taller and more elongated than those of Xenia, documented at 11–25 cm in A. glauca on wild reefs.

Growth form: Anthelia spreads by extending a flat, creeping mat (called a stolon) across rock and substrate surfaces, with new polyps emerging from that mat as the colony expands. This growth habit can be notably aggressive under ideal conditions; colonies have been observed overtaking large sections of rockwork within weeks to months. Placing frags on isolated rubble islands or removable frag plugs is generally recommended, so the colony can be trimmed back or repositioned without having to cut into established live rock.

Lighting: Anthelia is generally considered to do well under low-to-moderate light, commonly placed in the range of approximately 50–150 PAR (a measure of the light energy usable by photosynthetic organisms). Some acclimated aquarium strains have been reported to tolerate higher intensities, but new acquisitions are generally best started in lower-light positions and moved up gradually if the colony appears unhealthy.

Flow: Moderate, varied flow is generally preferred — enough to keep polyps gently waving and free of settled detritus, but not so turbulent that the colony is stressed. Some sources suggest moderate-to-high flow is also acceptable once a colony is established.

Water chemistry: Anthelia is considered relatively tolerant compared to stony (SPS) corals, making it well suited to tanks that cannot maintain the tightest reef chemistry. Calcium in the range of 400–450 ppm and magnesium in the range of 1300–1450 ppm are the values most commonly cited alongside this species.

Reef compatibility: Anthelia is generally regarded as reef-safe in that it does not actively sting neighboring corals with sweeper tentacles. Its primary compatibility concern is physical encroachment — the spreading stolon mat can overgrow and smother slower-growing neighbors if left unchecked. Providing buffer space around the colony and monitoring its expansion is the most effective management approach.
breedingNotes: Propagation in captivity is typically achieved by placing a new piece of rock or rubble adjacent to an established colony and allowing it to encrust naturally onto the new substrate over a period of days to weeks — a gentler method than direct fragging. Direct fragging by cutting polyp stalks is possible, but colonies are generally said to recover more readily when allowed to extend runners onto new substrate on their own. On natural reefs, A. glauca has been documented reproducing sexually through broadcast spawning timed to full moons in summer, with larvae briefly brooded internally before being released. Sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: Bulk Reef Supply – ECC Waving Hand Anthelia
primary.notes: Primary source used in place of LiveAquaria (which returned 404 for all Anthelia URL attempts) and WWC (which also returned 404). BRS is a listed secondary for coral-saltwater slice and was the most complete single source for water chemistry parameters (temperature 75–78°F, salinity 1.025–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–9.5 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1300–1450 ppm, PAR 150–250).

additional.name: Whitlyn Aquatics – Anthelia Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-verified temperature (74–80°F / 23–27°C), salinity (1.024–1.026), pH (8.1–8.4), moderate flow, beginner care level, photosynthetic diet, peaceful temperament. Confirmed Anthelia does not pulse unlike Xenia. Did not provide alkalinity, calcium, or magnesium values.

additional.name: AquariumDomain – Anthelia Waving Hand Coral Species Profile
additional.notes: Cross-verified temperature (72–78°F / 22–26°C), salinity (1.023–1.025), pH (8.1–8.4), alkalinity (8–12 dKH), moderate-to-strong flow, lower placement, Indo-Pacific range. Confirmed 8 pinnate tentacles per polyp and non-pulsing behavior. Minor disagreement on flow (moderate-to-strong vs moderate); also listed care level as moderate vs beginner majority.

additional.name: Reeflings – Anthelia glauca Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-verified temperature (24–28°C / 75–82°F), salinity (1.023–1.026), pH (8.0–8.4), PAR 70–150, moderate flow, bottom placement, fast growth, peaceful temperament. Did not provide alkalinity, calcium, or magnesium values.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Anthelia glauca
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family: Xeniidae, Order: Malacalcyonacea), native range (Indo-Pacific, Red Sea to Hawaii, 8–22 m depth), morphology (8 plume-like tentacles, polyps 11–25 cm tall, non-retractable, non-pulsing), and reproductive biology (broadcast spawning, dioecious, pharyngeal brooding). Not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Anthelia (coral genus taxonomy)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy confirmation (Family: Xeniidae, Order: Malacalcyonacea, Class: Octocorallia) and species list (13 known species including A. glauca, A. elongata, A. japonica, A. strumosa). Not used for husbandry.
